7 Milestones of the Buyer’s Journey (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 1 of 8
Speaker 1: Hey, guys. Welcome back to this lesson. This is where we are really going to go deeper
into what exactly that transformation is between point A and point buyer. I think any
journey or transformation sounds kind of vague until you qualify it, until you actually
really outline that map, outline the steps someone needs to take. This is where we're
really actually going to get a little more granular, and talk about what those key
milestones are along that buyer's journey, and how to start using our messaging to
generate momentum. Build that momentum, and move someone. Move a prospect
through that path between point A and point buyer.
Here is that path once again, and if you look at the bottom of our map here, you will see
that point A, the first milestone, is really validated. That's the entry ticket to getting a
prospect on this journey with us, is to make them feel validated in their current
experience. Get them to a place where they feel seen, heard, and understood. It's
because you've confirmed their current reality to them, including the things they're
already trying to do to achieve that outcome or overcome that pain. Reflecting that,
validating that, recognizing that is what makes them trust you enough to embark on
that journey. Trust you enough that you've taken all of them into consideration, and still
can promise this new, favorable outcome to them.
That's what we're doing here. And built into this initial phase of validating, of achieving
that first milestone, it's impossible to not have a conversation about your prospect's
level of awareness. These are the classic five levels of awareness by Gene Schwartz, no
relation. Not that I know of anyway. Essentially in the coaching online education space
that a lot of you are operating under, it's my experience that most of your prospects are
going to fall under the problem aware level. However, there is a slight caveat that really
fuels my initial research, my initial deep dive into an audience. It's leaning in with the
question, “Is anybody ever really problem aware?”
I never take for granted that my prospect is even problem aware. In fact, even if they
are classically considered to be problem aware. I like to approach my campaigns with
this beginner's mind and tap into what I consider to be a hidden sixth stage of
awareness, or a sixth level. That is symptom aware. No one really have a true grasp on
the source of their problem. They have an idea, they may have a pre-cognition of it, a
conception, but especially in the online space, in the online education space: coaching,
consulting. The root problem is your job as the expert, to elucidate, to educate, and
that's a very important gift you can give them especially at the beginning of a funnel
where you're validating their experience and giving them that gift of diagnosis.
It's like when you go to a doctor and saying, this hurts and that hurts. “My, my, stomach
has been hurting me for weeks.” That's a symptom, that's not a problem. An
inexperienced doctor or a friend might consider your stomach hurting to be the
problem, but that's actually symptomatic of something much deeper. The truth is,
ultimately, every pain point is both a problem and a symptom. If you really want to
make your product, your course, your program the only logical choice, it all starts with
diagnosing the real problem.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 8 --

7 Milestones of the Buyer’s Journey (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 2 of 8
So operate from a place that your prospect is symptomatically aware, not problem
aware, and then give them that gift of diagnosis. That's your job, as their educator, your
job is to give that gift of perfect diagnosis, and we'll show you how to do that in your
messaging once we get into the pre-launch sequence.
For now, I just want you to consider this secret sixth stage, because that is where I've
operated in every launch I've done for clients, is really tapping into how can I turn what
my prospect considers to be a problem and actually make it symptomatic of a more root
problem that we're uniquely capable of solving. Because, that's a very powerful
relationship you can create with your audience. That is how you build authority. It's not
just about having articles up on Forbes, on Entrepreneur, on Inc., or whatever the main
publications of your industry are. Anybody could do that. But being able to expertly
diagnose, and peel back, and say, “No, that's actually a symptom of something deeper.”
That is really how you not just tell people you're in authority with some media logos but
actually show it, display it without having to boast.
This is how that process goes for that hidden sixth stage, and we will get into an
exercise. I'll walk you through an exercise a little later on in this module on how to
uncover it. It's essentially taking what they perceive, what your audience perceives to be
a problem, taking a few steps back and really saying, “No, that's actually a symptom,
which gives you the right to re-diagnose the real problem.” If backed up by believable
data or case studies, it actually makes you the sole authority to solve it. You've created
this new relationship between your prospect and the problem they're feeling, and that
gives you the unique right to solve it in your way.
That is one major thing that I really credit as being probably the most powerful shift I've
made, as a copywriter, as a marketer, is to just not take people's perceived problems for
granted. And actually hold their hand as we dive deeper and make it a symptom instead
of a problem, and then unearth, root out the real problem, which makes us really be
only people capable of solving it. That's how you create a blue ocean around your
product. You really change the problem. You redefine the problem in a way that only
you could solve it.
There's one other thing I want to talk about while we're still on the topic of Uncle Gene,
and that's the difference between levels of awareness and depth of awareness. This is
something that probably wasn't as relevant to be talking about back in the '50s, or '60s,
or '70s, or '80s, or even the '90s. But right now when our attention is so scattered and
pulled at by so many different areas, you know, it's not enough to really just focus on
what is your prospect's level of awareness. You really need to ramp up and amplify their
depth of awareness.
You know, just because someone is symptomatically aware or problem aware doesn't
mean their awareness over it is strong, or consistent enough for them to be motivated
enough to move through it. So just as it's important to identify the level of awareness,
holding awareness over it long enough to get them to act is also a part of your job as a
skillful marketer, or a skillful conversion coach. It's like reflecting sun through a
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 8 --

7 Milestones of the Buyer’s Journey (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 3 of 8
magnifying glass. You really need to hold it there long enough until it catches fire, until
that problem really starts to burn, until that symptom is really that main focus.
It's really holding that focus, that depth of awareness. And to do this, I like to marry our
good friend Gene Schwartz with a little guy named Freud. Definitely no relation to
Freud, and we're just using his name to break down these five points, if you will. This is
how I ramp up that depth of awareness. This is something that really gets dialed in over
the course of your funnel, and it all starts with frequency. This is why during that
intensive period of a launch, or when someone enters your funnel, you want to be
mailing them daily. You want them to see you at different points. This is why we do
retargeting. This is why you'll send emails with great frequency, but you'll also send
maybe a text as a reminder if somebody registered for your webinar. That's why you'll
also try to send retargeting ads, to just stay top of mind.
Frequency is really important. It's not enough to just capture attention, you have to stay
in front of them long enough to hold it, or at least during that period of transformation.
We're not saying that you're going to be in front of them eight times a day for the next
year. We're saying be in front of them as frequently as possible during that one week,
two week, three week period of your launch. Because when you think about it, if you're
trying to create an incredible personal transformation for yourself, and you're working
with a coach, or you're working with a therapist, what do you do?
A lot of people go on those weekend intensives, where you're literally spending 72
straight hours with a group. It has that frequency effect. If you're really trying to work
on a new habit, or behavior, or overcoming some sort of trauma, you might have a lot of
sessions in a really short span, because you're really focused on trying to create that
transformation.
The other thing is repetition. Practically speaking, this means choosing a few powerful
sales arguments, and beliefs, and reframes to make across your funnel instead of
throwing around more and more and more to see what sticks. As we get deeper into the
course, especially during the sales module in module six, you'll see what we're actually
doing there. You'll see how we're really setting up those arguments in the pre-launch,
and reinforcing them in the sales phases instead of creating new ones. The goal is really
to repeat them, strengthen them, reinforce them, and re-contextualize them, instead of
diluting and muddying the pool with just too many ideas.
The other thing is being evocative. That captures attention. That means using story,
using word pictures, really leveraging specificity in your copy to create this vivid image
in your prospect's mind. You know, what captures our attention, what captures the
depth of attention is when we actually use more of our faculties to relate with it. Our
brains don't know the difference between an imagined experience and a real one. So if
you can walk them through and really be evocative in your words, that's what gets your
prospects to play full, to increase that depth of awareness, that depth of attention.
That's something that is extremely strong and something you want to capitalize on over
the course of that funnel.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

Page 4 of 8
The other one is unexpected. That's your classic pattern interrupt. We're going to learn
how to do this right off the bat early in our funnel, by calling it the sacred cows. That
could be a little bit contrarian, that gets the ears perking. So it's really curiosity driven,
but it's also a good idea to know what your prospect is seeing. How they've been trying
to solve the problem, so you can effectively position against that white noise. So you
can call B.S. on the things that they're aware of, and really draw that attention. So,
unexpected sometimes means being challenging. It's just anything that really separates
and distances from the white noise of your market, and gets that attention.
Because, once they just hook on to the fact that you're approaching things in a different
unexpected way, that's what's going to earn you the right to kind of ... to earn the right
to continue communicating with them and leveraging that attention. That's a big one.
Then finally, probably the most powerful one especially for the coaches, the experts, the
educators, the trainers, listening to this, but be diagnostic. The way I like to illustrate
this is, think of when you as a normal human, not the entrepreneurial version of you, is
the most focused and dialed in and present with what someone is saying. It might not
be your spouse, or your partner when they're talking about their day, but if you just
went through a series of medical testing for something, and you had those 15 minutes
sitting in front of a doctor when they come back into the room, and they have those
results. They're about to dive into it with you, and tell you exactly what is up. You're
paying attention. Your ears are perked up, and you are focusing on every word.
The more diagnostic you could be across your funnel, especially at the beginning, that is
how time almost becomes suspended, that's how you become the most important
person in their life in that moment. We're talking about diagnosing everything,
diagnosing previous failures, diagnosing their last action. You know, if they clicked on a
link, what does that mean? If they signed up for a webinar but didn't attend, what does
that mean about what's going on in their life? So definitely diagnose wherever possible.
That's what creates those, “Oh my god, he gets me,” moments, when you can reflect
back and diagnose exactly why someone did something, because they haven't actually
done any of the self-awareness to know why they did it themselves.
That is extremely powerful, and that really blends well with validating their initial
starting point, and what they're seeing. That comes down to diagnosis. That's really a
loaded deep overview of that first milestone. As we go deeper, the second one is simply
excitement around the outcome. Most afflicted prospects will start off with the sense of
disillusionment and skepticism around solving the problem, or achieving the outcome.
But if you've validated their reality and diagnosed why they haven't succeeded in the
past, it opens up this small window of hope. The shell cracks just wide enough to get
your prospect excited about the outcome again.
They may still be skeptical ,and that's okay. They're allowed to be skeptical, but we have
their attention, and we have their excitement, which is really what we're going for at
that stage of the customer journey. There are other ways to build that excitement, and
we're going to talk more about them as we get into module three. One of them is a
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 8 --

7 Milestones of the Buyer’s Journey (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 5 of 8
concept I call exclusive empowerment, and this is where you give your prospect the
chance to cash in on their privilege points.
We all have this drive to make the most out of our potential and opportunity. No one
wants to leave privilege points on the table. This is where you give them a chance to
leverage it, leverage their current skills or circumstantial elements. If your method or
what you're going to do is only going to work for entrepreneurs who've earned over
150K in the past year, that's something they're proud of. That's something they want to
leverage. It's like a velvet rope policy but taken to a deeper more meaningful level, and
we'll get more into that later.
In Joseph Campbell's Hero's Journey, this is where we would call them to adventure.
There's a sense of being the chosen one, and it has to be them. They've been selected,
and that's a very powerful trigger to elicit excitement. It is exciting leveraging
opportunities that are given to you.
The other thing we're really setting up here is this sense of it's not your fault until it is.
This is a big theme I've used in every single launch, you know, what gets people re-
engaged and excited about an outcome is the transference of blame or self-judgment
that comes along with it. They may have been embarrassed or ashamed that they're still
spinning their wheels on a problem, but part of your job, especially in the early going,
it's to show them why it wasn't their fault in a believable way that they could actually
buy in to.
This is what takes the energy out of self-blame and allows them to channel it back into
resourcefulness and excitement. It gives you a chance, more importantly, to keep them
accountable towards the end. This is why I call it the, "It's not your fault until it is,"
principle, because once they see the light, once they buy into it and commit to it, it's
their responsibility to act on it and it's your responsibility as a conversion coach to
challenge them to stay consistent with that.
Once we have that excitement, we want to get our prospect committed to that new
outcome, and we want to get that commitment in the most friction-free part of your
funnel. That's the important note here. There's no offer yet at this stage of the buyer's
journey, only the promise of an exciting outcome, only forgiveness over past failures.
That creates hope that it's achievable and a commitment towards achieving it. This is
exactly what you want to dial in way before your offer comes into play. You want to get
that commitment to the outcome before there's a chance to resist, before there's even
a notion of pulling out their wallet. That's a big one that we're going to talk a lot about
when we look at the confirmation stage.
Next thing, belief in outcome. That's the next big milestone, it's this steadfast belief that
the outcome is possible. Excitement and commitment can indeed exist before the
unshakable belief that it's possible, and that's something we call hope. They hope it will
happen, but by this stage in your timeline, after you've given the arguments, and the
reasons why it's possible, we are turning that inspiration to aspiration. The dream has
become more lucid. They can project themselves in that future reality or outcome, not
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 8 --

7 Milestones of the Buyer’s Journey (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 6 of 8
just all the way on the other end of the spectrum but actually doing it. Taking the
tangible, practical steps that your program will later facilitate in order to make that
outcome possible.
We do this through a series of ahas, insights, beliefs, and reframes. The prelaunch is
really instrumental in delivering this, and just making it more aspirational, going from
inspiration to aspiration, which is really a more active force. They see this new version of
themselves not on an abstract level, not as an abstract ideal, but a tangible, real,
possibilities. They have this version of themselves to grow into, which your product will
later facilitate. This is a critical juncture, and it's our marketing that's giving them the gift
of a believable and attainable aspiration.
This brings us to the next stage, which is the inevitability of the outcome. This is where
the journey hits a tipping point, and you'll see that on the process map. It's where we
start to have that downward slope. This is where the belief that it's possible turns into
an inevitable outcome. The reason being, because your program with its unique
features and mechanisms, finally comes into play and gives that aspiration a clear
strategy and path to manifest. And because they've already pre-committed to achieving
it prior in your launch, and have been given the full understanding of why they haven't
achieved it in the past, they're fueled by this renewed belief that it's possible, and your
program acts as the final piece to make it inevitable.
However, even though this is where we hit that tipping point, not everyone will buy
here. The transformation and outcome may be inevitable but for most it will still be
redoubled resistance, some last minute self-sabotage. You know that, “This won't work
for me,” attitude. That special snowflake syndrome, if you will. There's going to be that
fear of change, you know, investing and buying new products and changing yourself,
even in an aspirational way is fearful. We're not wired to want to do that. Resistance
and skepticism are going to come in strong here, and in Joseph Campbell parlance that
would be the dark knight of the soul moment.
This is where most marketers will counterintuitively just try to scare them off by
throwing more scarcity and formol into the fire, which has its place but it doesn't
address what your prospect really needs here. They're already in this dark loathsome
place, really wrestling with this negativity, wrestling with this resistance. There is that
place for real urgency, but it's not the only thing, and it's definitely not the only thing
that they will need in this moment. What they would really need is safety in the new
outcome. And on an internal level this is where future pacing would be extremely
helpful, for them to see what it would look like in the minutes, days, weeks, months,
and years after they've stepped into that new reality, because there's safety and
predictability.
So we seek to do our customer or our prospect the favor of removing the fear of the
unknown. We make it known. We future pace what it's going to look like, and in doing
so, we make the investment feel safe. When we're making the investment feel safe we
go beyond just a standard guarantee, social proof, and case studies. Sure we use those,
but we also tap into some deeper things that create safety for people. One of the big
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 8 --

7 Milestones of the Buyer’s Journey (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 7 of 8
ones and most underutilized one is we re-empower them to make a choice. We give
them a chance to not get pushed past the buyer's line, which is scary and something
they would resist but to actually take that step themselves by their own volition, by
taking your hand and making that step themselves.
Then module seven, we'll be looking at some very specific techniques and strategies to
do that.
Then finally, vindication, swiped from my favorite movie of all time. I call this the Andy
Dufresne moment, and this picture is kind of better, but most people who've seen this
movie will recognize the one with his arms flailing, rain pouring down on him, and just
this awesome moment in cinematic history. That looks like a vindicated smile, so we're
going to role with that for now.
One of the greatest gifts we can give our prospect is that sense of vindication of
redemption. Whereas we start the journey by verifying and validating their current
reality, by the end of the process they should feel this awesome sense of vindication.
That all aspects of their past, the failures, the doubt, the resistance, the stress, it's all
okay. It's all led to this moment. It's a powerful gift we can give them. It's been given
meaning. The pain has been of use, it hasn't been wasted.
Think of the line a lot of husbands or wives may give each other when they're talking
about their partner, “I'm so happy I finally, finally met you.” That word finally is the
vindication, it points to all the pain and missteps they've had in previous relationships,
that it's all okay now. It's been alchemized into a prerequisite for this final success. This
is a really powerful trigger, and we're going to talk a lot more about it when we get
towards the end of the course. If you have the six-figure emails package, there's an
additional template called, markets search for meaning, which was actually inspired by
Viktor Frankl. His process of Logotherapy, of turning these struggles into those
prerequisites for success. So, just reframing them in a new favorable way that vindicates
them.
That's the big one, but at the end of the day giving meaning to struggle is one of the
most generous gifts we can give another human, and now, we have a chance to do it at
scale with email marketing. It's the ultimate win-win. It's the culmination of this entire
process, and I really can't wait for you to start leveraging it.
Then finally, the last part I want to really kind of hone in on here is you'll see that there
are three state checks that we use strategically throughout this coaching to conversion
process. This is essentially the equivalent of when you're having that one on one
coaching call or a therapy session, and they will ask you. They will get your buy-in again.
They will get reaffirmation, “Is it okay if I talk about this? Do you want to hear about
this? Are you ready to go deeper into this?”
It's really getting that invitation again. We spoke before about permission versus
invitation marketing. One powerful way to do it is to just ask for that invitation, a few
times. It's just kind of like courtesy and makes people feel like they have control over it.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 8 --

7 Milestones of the Buyer’s Journey (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 8 of 8
That was another big element we just spoke about a little earlier, is giving people that
sense of power, that sense of control, giving them a chance to exercise their own
volition and strategic points.
You're going to see how we do that a little later, state check one is all about making sure
they're really on board. So that's an email I will send out during the segmentation phase,
especially if they haven't signed up for the journey over the first one or two invites.
You'll see a template on how to do that.
Then one that I really love leveraging is state check two, right to before, cart open,
getting permission to talk about it. So that's exactly when a coach would say, “I've heard
everything you've said, I think I have something that can really help. Can I tell you about
it?” That's what makes people feel more empowered. It's that micro-commitment. It's
that buy-in.
Then finally, we do it in the close, when we're actually trying to get a decision, and you'll
see a template for that as well. I just want to leave you with this thought, you know, one
of the biggest discrepancies between personal selling and online selling is that one on ...
is that in one on one the process must end in a choice. If you're doing your follow-ups, it
doesn't end with people just kind of falling off the radar. If you're really following up
with people it has to end in a choice, whereas in online marketing it ends with a
whimper, you know, in action and a passing deadline, which just isn't very informative
or valuable for either party. That's really what the final state check is going to help us
with, really asking for that decision.
That's it. That is the journey at a glance. And before we can expect our avatar to embark
on it, it's really time to give them that feat, give them a heartbeat, make them real, and
to take them off the page as a two-dimensional static thing sitting in a Google Doc, and
make them real and vivid, and pretty much that best friend you met at Starbucks last
weekend.
So, take a break. This was a really big lesson, steep in it for a little bit, and then meet me
in lesson two where we'll actually explore a few really cool exercises to bring your
prospect to life. I will see you there.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
